DiagramRecommendation.description: A single recommendation for a diagram to add to a report section.

diagram_type.description: One of the supported DIAGRAM_TYPES keys
diagram_type.title: Diagram Type

description.description: What the diagram should show, grounded in the section content
description.title: Description

DiagramRecommendation.title: DiagramRecommendation

schema_json.description: Full analysis output: which sections deserve diagrams.

recommendations.description: 2-5 sections that would benefit from a visual diagram
recommendations.title: Recommendations

schema_json.title: DiagramAnalysisResult

system:
You are a research report visualization specialist. You identify which report sections benefit most from visual diagrams and select the appropriate diagram type for each.

Available diagram types:
- "process_flow": How X works (step-by-step process)
- "comparison_matrix": X vs Y vs Z comparison
- "causal_chain": A causes B which leads to C
- "hierarchy": Classification/taxonomy of findings
- "timeline": Chronological development of topic
- "pros_cons": Advantages vs disadvantages
- "decision_tree": If X then Y, else Z

Output format example:
{"recommendations": [{"section_id": "s01", "section_title": "Mechanisms of Action", "diagram_type": "process_flow", "description": "Step-by-step process of how compound X binds to receptor Y, triggers pathway Z, and produces effect W as described in the section"}]}
prompt:
Given these report sections, identify 2-5 that would benefit from a visual diagram. For each, specify the diagram type and what the diagram should show.

SECTIONS:
--- Section s01: Fundamentals of Damp Heat Aging in Polymeric Encapsulants (449 words) ---
Damp heat aging represents a critical degradation pathway for polymeric encapsulants used in flexible electronics, where the simultaneous presence of elevated temperature and humidity drives accelerated interfacial failure. During damp heat exposure, higher temperature increases both the chemical reaction rate and the moisture diffusion coefficient, promoting greater water penetration into adhesive joints and further causing aging and degradation [REF:1]. This dual mechanism means that temperature acts as a kinetic accelerator for moisture-mediated damage at the encapsulant-substrate interface.

The severity of thermal degradation at adhesive interfaces warrants attention even in the absence of moisture-related stressors. Thermal degradation of the encapsulant-substrate interface can be significant even without UV exposure and elevated humidity [REF:2], indicating that temperature alone constitutes an independent degradation vector. When thermal cycling is combined with humidity, as in standard damp heat protocols (85 degC / 85% RH), the degradation rates observed exceed those attributable to either factor in isolation [REF:1][REF:2].

Material selection plays a decisive role in determining the extent of thermally induced adhesion loss. Thermal aging by 5,000 thermocycles induced a significant decrease in micro-tensile bond strength values for all seven adhesive systems tested [REF:3]. Among the systems evaluated, one-step self-etch adhesives showed the lowest post-aging bond [...]

--- Section s02: Adhesion Degradation Kinetics at Encapsulant-Glass and Encapsulant-Cell Interfaces (846 words) ---
Accelerated aging protocols provide quantitative benchmarks for assessing adhesion degradation at critical encapsulant interfaces. At 10,000 hours of accelerated aging, EVA adhesion in mini-modules falls to a level generally seen after more than 15 years of field exposure [REF:6]. This finding establishes a direct temporal bridge between laboratory and field conditions, indicating that standard test durations well below 10,000 hours capture only early-stage degradation behavior and may underestimate the full trajectory of adhesion loss over a module's operational lifetime [REF:6].

Interfacial degradation does not proceed uniformly across all bonded surfaces within a photovoltaic stack. The glass/EVA interface experiences more degradation than the EVA/cell interface [REF:5], and this differential degradation rate explains why the glass/EVA boundary -- despite being the initially stronger interface -- becomes the preferential site for delamination [REF:5]. This reversal of relative interfacial strength has direct implications for module reliability: failure progresses where the degradation rate is highest rather than where the initial adhesion is weakest [REF:5][REF:6].

Parallel findings from adhesive bond strength testing across different substrates reinforce the principle that substrate type governs degradation behavior. Three-way ANOVA revealed that substrate type (p=0.00) and aging method (p=0.00) had statistically significant effects on bond strength [REF:8]. By contrast [...]

--- Section s03: Encapsulant Material Comparison: EVA, POE, TPO, and EPE under Damp Heat Stress (573 words) ---
Comparative performance under accelerated aging reveals a divergent ranking of encapsulant adhesion stability depending on whether the test is conducted at coupon level or at module level under damp heat conditions. POE encapsulant coupons demonstrated the best adhesion stability under thermal aging, followed by EPE then EVA [REF:9]. This coupon-level hierarchy positions polyolefin elastomers as the most thermally durable option among the three chemistries tested, with EVA ranking last in thermal adhesion retention [REF:9].

At the module level under damp heat conditions, however, the ranking partially inverts. EVA laminates showed less peel force degradation after damp heat than POE samples [REF:10]. This discordance between coupon-level thermal aging and module-level damp heat performance indicates that moisture exposure introduces a distinct degradation pathway that penalizes POE more severely than dry thermal stress alone [REF:9][REF:10]. The difference may relate to lamination processing: a higher lamination temperature was beneficial for peel force and gel content before and after aging for cross-linking encapsulants [REF:10], suggesting that EVA's crosslinked network -- when properly processed at elevated lamination temperature -- confers improved moisture resistance at the module level.

Both hot-dry and hot-humid aging protocols produce measurable adhesion loss across all three encapsulant chemistries. Adhesion energy Gc decreased for EVA, POE, and EPE encapsulants a [...]

--- Section s04: Influence of Vinyl Acetate Content and Crosslinking Degree on DH Resistance (775 words) ---
Vinyl acetate (VA) content exerts a decisive influence on the mechanical and optical durability of PEVA encapsulants under damp heat stress. The PV industry consensus holds that PEVA encapsulant should have VA content between 28% and 33% [REF:11], a range historically selected to balance transmittance, flexibility, and lamination processability. Systematic damp heat aging experiments across a broader VA range (18-40%) reveal, however, that this consensus range does not optimize for long-term durability.

Tensile strength, thermal stability, and degree of crosslinking were found to be greater for PEVA containing lower VA content (18% and 24%) and diminished for PEVA with higher VA content (33% and 40%) after damp heat aging [REF:11]. This inverse relationship between VA content and post-aging mechanical performance is pronounced: PEVA with higher VA content (33% and 40%) degraded significantly after 2,000 hours of damp heat aging, resulting in significant loss in transmittance and mechanical integrity [REF:11]. The degradation pattern indicates that higher VA fractions increase the polymer's susceptibility to moisture-induced chain scission and deacetylation, accelerating both optical yellowing and mechanical embrittlement under combined thermal-humidity stress.

The optimal VA content is not simply the lowest value tested. PEVA with 18% VA, despite its superior tensile strength and crosslinking retention, has inadequate transmittance and relatively high stiffness after 2,000  [...]

--- Section s05: Lamination Process Parameters and Their Effect on Long-Term Adhesion Retention (876 words) ---
Industry benchmarks for post-lamination adhesion define the performance floor against which process parameter effects must be evaluated. EVA manufacturer STR recommends a minimum of 53 N/cm peel strength after lamination, measured by 180-degree peel test, to ensure a 20-year module lifetime [REF:14]. In parallel, threshold debonding energy values of 160 J/m2 for encapsulant interfaces and 10 J/m2 for backsheet interfaces have been proposed [REF:14]. These dual criteria -- force-per-width and fracture energy -- establish independent quantitative targets that link initial lamination quality to long-term field durability.

Lamination temperature is a primary process lever for achieving durable adhesion. PV modules laminated at elevated temperatures up to 180 degC withstood DH3000 damp heat aging tests in terms of peel force and yellowing [REF:13]. This finding demonstrates that higher lamination temperatures produce encapsulant-substrate bonds capable of surviving 3,000 hours of damp heat without falling below functional thresholds for either mechanical adhesion or optical degradation [REF:13]. The mechanism linking elevated lamination temperature to improved durability operates through enhanced crosslink formation: higher cure temperatures drive the peroxide-initiated crosslinking reaction toward greater gel content, producing a denser polymer network that restricts moisture diffusion and chain mobility under subsequent damp heat exposure [REF:13][REF:14].

However, an upper bo [...]

--- Section s06: Moisture Diffusion, Hydrolytic Degradation, and Interfacial Chemistry (529 words) ---
Surface-analytical characterization of damp-heat-exposed laminate fracture surfaces reveals the chemical fingerprints of interfacial degradation. XPS detected carboxylic acid groups on fractured encapsulant surfaces and deprotonated silanol and water bands on the opposite glass sides of interfacially fractured EVA and POE laminates [REF:15]. The simultaneous presence of carboxylic acid moieties on the polymer face and deprotonated silanol on the glass face indicates hydrolytic cleavage of the silane coupling agent bonds that bridge the encapsulant-glass interface [REF:15]. This degradation pathway is specific to damp heat exposure: the siloxane bonds (Si-O-Si) linking the organosilane primer to the glass surface are susceptible to nucleophilic attack by water molecules, producing silanol (Si-OH) groups and liberating the organic coupling chain as carboxylic acid fragments detectable on the polymer side [REF:15].

Bulk spectroscopic evidence provides a critical distinction between thermal and hydrothermal degradation pathways. FTIR-ATR analysis showed insignificant bulk encapsulant degradation under thermal aging alone, demonstrating the critical role of UV and moisture in accelerating degradation [REF:9]. This finding establishes that the polymer matrix itself remains relatively intact under dry thermal stress -- the degradation observed at the interface [REF:15] requires moisture as a co-factor rather than being driven by thermal energy alone [REF:9]. The convergence of thes [...]

--- Section s07: Industry Standards, Quality Thresholds, and Field Failure Rates (529 words) ---
Qualification testing under combined thermal and humidity stress provides the primary mechanism for evaluating encapsulant durability before field deployment. High temperature and high humidity combined cause accelerated joint deterioration through the damp heat aging effect, where elevated temperature increases both the chemical reaction rate and the diffusion coefficient, promoting more water diffusion that causes aging and degradation affecting mechanical properties of bonded joints [REF:1]. This dual acceleration mechanism forms the physical basis for standardized damp heat test protocols used in module certification.

Recent large-scale qualification data reveal that current damp heat testing exposes significant durability deficiencies across the industry. Only 69% of bills of materials (BOMs) tested degraded by less than 6% after damp heat testing [REF:17]. The DH failure rate doubled compared to the 2023 Scorecard, with 15 manufacturers experiencing at least one failure during damp heat testing [REF:17]. Failure modes included delamination, junction box failures, electrical insulation faults, and power loss [REF:17]. The year-over-year doubling of failure rates indicates a worsening trend in module durability under standard DH qualification, raising questions about whether material or process changes introduced for cost reduction have compromised long-term reliability.

Post-DH2000 delamination provides a concrete example of how adhesion failure manifests in practice.  [...]

--- Section s08: Implications for DVS-PEI Encapsulant Design in Flexible Electronics (577 words) ---
The combined effect of temperature and humidity on adhesive joint integrity provides the central design constraint for DVS-PEI encapsulants operating in damp heat environments. Tensile strength of high-temperature curing epoxy adhesive joints decreased by approximately 31.3% after moisture absorption alone and by 27.7% under high temperature (95 degC) dry conditions [REF:1]. Under combined high temperature and humid conditions, the decrease reached 54.4% [REF:1] -- exceeding the sum of individual effects (31.3% + 27.7% = 59.0%, though the combined loss of 54.4% demonstrates that the two degradation pathways interact rather than acting independently). This synergistic degradation profile [REF:1] establishes that encapsulant adhesion design must account for the simultaneous presence of heat and moisture rather than treating each stressor in isolation.

Accelerated aging protocols provide a standardized framework for evaluating encapsulant formulations against this combined threat. PEVA films with four different VA content levels (18%, 24%, 33%, and 40%) were fabricated with necessary additives, cured at 150 degC under vacuum, and then subjected to accelerated damp heat aging at 85 degC and 85% relative humidity according to IEC 61215 for durations of 1,000 and 2,000 hours [REF:11]. This experimental design -- spanning a wide compositional range under standardized IEC conditions -- provides a methodological template applicable to DVS-PEI formulation screening [REF:11]. The IEC 6 [...]

Constraints:
- Select at most 5 sections.
- Only recommend a diagram if it adds genuine clarity beyond the text.
- The description must reference specific facts or structures present in the section content.
- Choose the diagram type that best fits the section's analytical structure.
